coredata.dc:title: Positive carbon dots with dual roles of nanoquencher and reference signal for the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of DNA
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Rubin
Chen, Bing
Li, Fenglan
Weng, Shaohuang
Zheng, Zongfu
Chen, Min
Wu, Wen
Lin, Xinhua
Yang, Chaoyong
coredata.dc:description: Specific modification of carbon dots (CDs) exhibit excellent properties and are suitable for numerous applications. The exploitation of multifunctional CDs in the area of fluorescent assay is still urgent and attractive. Herein, using a simple two-step heating procedure, positive charged carbon dots (P-CDs) were prepared with the co-doping of the elements of N and S. The morphology, optical properties and stability of P-CDs was investigated. Furthermore, P-CDs show the dual functions of unprecedented quenching performance toward fluorophore-modified DNA coupling the retaining fluorescence of P-CDs self. The quenching effect and mechanism of P-CDs to fluorophore-modified DNA was investigated. The fluorescence and quenching properties of P-CDs were used to design a ratiometric fluorescence platform for DNA based on the complex of P-CDs and FAM-labeled ssDNA. The ratiometric method allows the detection of target DNA because of the different affinities of P-CDs to ssDNA and dsDNA with acceptable sensitivity and improved reproducibility, when using sequence fragment of the gene of Gardnerella vaginalis as model. This method provides a new approach to widen the future of the biological applications based on some special CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Positive carbon dots
Fluorescense quenching
DNA probe
Dual functions
Ratiometric biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of positive charged carbon dots (P-CDs)
DNA detection assisted with exonuclease III (Exo III) amplification using P-CDs as nanoquencher

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis of P-CDs in a two-step procedure; (B) The quenching of fluorophore-labelled ssDNA by P-CDs, while retaining the stable fluorescence intensity of P-CDs, enabling a ratiometric analytical method for Gardnerella vaginalis DNA with the target sequence circulating under the assistance of Exo III.

TEM image (A); AFM image and height profile analysis (B), FTIR (C), XPS (D), UV–vis and fluorescence spectra (E) and appearance in solid state (a) or aqueous dispersion (500 μg/mL) in daylight (b) and UV excitation (c) (F) of P-CDs. Inset in (A) shows the size distribution (upper) and lattice spacing (under) of P-CDs.

Quenching behavior of different contents P-CDs to (A) 50 nM FAM-labeled ssDNA and (B) ROX-labeled ATP-binding aptamer probe in Tris-HCl. (C) Quenching efficiency of 6.25 μg/mL P-CDs to probes with different lengths and compositions, and (D) Fluorescence spectra of 50 nM pure FAM in the absence and presence of 6.25 μg/mL P-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of P-DNA with and without P-CDs in pure water. (B) Zeta-potential of P-CDs with and without P-DNA in pure water. (C) TEM image of the assembly of P-CDs when P-DNA is added. (D) Circular dichroism spectra of 1 μM P-DNA with the content of GO or different concentrations of P-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of 50 nM P-DNA, 50 nM P-DNA with the addition of 6.25 μg/mL P-CDs, and the system of 50 nM P-DNA+ 6.25 μg/mL P-CDs hybridized with 250 nM C-DNA. (B) Fluorescence anisotropy of (a) 50 nM P-DNA and (b) 50 nM P-DNA + 250 nM C-DNA in the absence and presence of 6.25 μg/mL P-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the sensing platform containing 6.25 μg/mL P-CDs and 50 nM P-DNA in the presence of different concentrations of T-DNA. (B) Relationship and linear relationship (inset) of FFAM/FP-CDs vs concentrations of T-DNA.

Comparison of reproducibility between non-ratiometric strategy (A) and ratiometric strategy (B). The black histograms represent the values of 30 individual measurements. Average value and standard deviation (SD) for 30 individual measurements are represented by red bars and red error bars, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Positive carbon dots with dual roles of nanoquencher and reference signal for the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of DNA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, The Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Fujian Province, State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces, Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials, Department of Chemical Engineering, Department of Chemical Biology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation
The Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Fujian Province
State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces
Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Fujian Medical University Union Hospital, Fuzhou 350001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Specific modification of carbon dots (CDs) exhibit excellent properties and are suitable for numerous applications. The exploitation of multifunctional CDs in the area of fluorescent assay is still urgent and attractive. Herein, using a simple two-step heating procedure, positive charged carbon dots (P-CDs) were prepared with the co-doping of the elements of N and S. The morphology, optical properties and stability of P-CDs was investigated. Furthermore, P-CDs show the dual functions of unprecedented quenching performance toward fluorophore-modified DNA coupling the retaining fluorescence of P-CDs self. The quenching effect and mechanism of P-CDs to fluorophore-modified DNA was investigated. The fluorescence and quenching properties of P-CDs were used to design a ratiometric fluorescence platform for DNA based on the complex of P-CDs and FAM-labeled ssDNA. The ratiometric method allows the detection of target DNA because of the different affinities of P-CDs to ssDNA and dsDNA with acceptable sensitivity and improved reproducibility, when using sequence fragment of the gene of Gardnerella vaginalis as model. This method provides a new approach to widen the future of the biological applications based on some special CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials have drawn considerable attention in recent years due to their chemical stability, optical properties, photo-application and biocompatibility [1–5]. Among fluorescent carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs) are being increasingly recognized for their broad promising applications [6–8]. Recently, several effective methods for the accurate control of surface chemistry to obtain CDs with desirable characteristics, such as long wavelength emission and high quantum yield (QY), had been developed for biomedical application [7,8]. However, further investigation should be conducted on the preparation and novel property exposure with special application of CDs, for example the interaction between CDs and DNA, which can be an important factor for DNA sensing. In order to expand the application of CDs in DNA detection, some new and effective synthetic approaches for the fabrication of CDs with new and enhanced properties are still needed.

In the area of DNA determination, increasing attention is drawn to the design of rational and sensitive biosensing strategy based on the interaction of DNA and nanomaterials [9–12]. Carbon-based materials [13], metal and metal compounds [14–17], metal–organic framework (MOF) [18] and polymers (for example polydopamine) [19] were applied as smart carrier and quencher for dyes modified single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) to form a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) pair [18,19]. Then, a fluorescent sensing platform through the different binding force (that include π-π stacking and or hydrophobic interactions) of nanomaterials towards ssDNA and DNA duplex (dsDNA) was constructed [19]. In these designs, the construction of the quenching nanomaterials, also called nanoquencher, was the focus to obtain different affinities to different forms of DNA (ssDNA, Hairpin structure DNA and dsDNA). The sensing principles were on the basis of the specific action between the quenched DNA probe and target biomolecules [20,21]. Althouth numerous nanoquenchers for the fluoresecent sensing platform for DNA were fabricated [13–19], the function of the nanoquenchers was normally a carrier. The design and exploitation of new kind nanoquencher with special property for the construction of novel fluorescent sensing platforms for biomolecules based on DNA sequence as probe with improved performance for expanded application is still demanded.

For the wide and facile real application of DNA biosensor, it requires the performance of the method sensitive, reproducible and stable, especially for the performance of reproducibility. Among various fluorescent methods, ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy is an effective strategy that detects two fluorescence signals with different emission wavelengths in a ratio form. Such routine can eliminate possible interfering effects from environmental factors, including intensity and stability of instrument, to increase reproducibility, accuracy and stability of testing result [22–26]. Recent reported works about ratiometric fluorescent platform were focused on the synthesis of bioprobes or dual signals system [22–25] for various targets [22–26]. Moreover, for the detection of specific genes or small biomolecule (ATP), some ratiometric electrochemical strategies were fabricated through dual labeled signals [27–30]. However, using fluorescence or electrochemical strategy, a few works about the sensing of DNA through ratiometric methods were just based on single labeled signal. It is known that nanomaterials including carbon-based materials can be used as effective nanoquenchers for the construction of FRET sensing platforms for genes, proteins and metal ions. However, nanomaterials used as platform in fluorescence sensing methods merely function as carriers [18,19], the other inherent properties of nanomaterials, such as redox and luminescence, have been ignored [16,10].

Heteroatom doping into CDs is an effective approach to improve even exploit new properties of CDs [1]. Herein, ratiometric fluorescence sensing of DNA based on positively charged fluorescent N,S co-doped CDs (P-CDs) with the dual roles of nanoquencher and reference signal was developed. As shown in Scheme 1 , P-CDs with amine-rich surface were produced by a simple two-step procedure. The obtained P-CDs possessed long-time fluorescence stability with acceptable quantum yield and high photobleaching resistance. P-CDs display powerful quenching ability toward fluorophore-modified DNA, while retaining the inherent luminescence of P-CDs, suggesting that they can serve as a new biointerface for DNA sensing through hybridizing with complementary DNA sequence. Herein, P-CDs play dual functions of quenching media for fluorophore-modified DNA and the stable fluorescence intensity as an internal reference signal for ratiometric method. The sequence fragment of the gene of Gardnerella vaginalis, the main pathogenic bacterium of bacterial nonspecific vaginitis, was selected as a model analyte to detect using the robust and versatile biosensing platform through ratiometric strategy with improved reproducibility (Scheme 1). This study offers new insights into the application of fluorescent carbon-based materials through the dual functions and expands the biosensing scope based on the interaction between DNA and nanomaterials.

Details on the materials, characterization methods, synthesis of citric acid CDs using pure citric acid as source were illustrated in supporting information. The quantum yield (QY) measurements, cytotoxicity assay, optimization of the concentration of P-CDs for the quenching of dye-labeled DNA, fluorescence quenching measurements of dye-labeled DNA using P-CDs, circular dichroism (CD) measurements, fluorescence anisotropy measurment, protective effect of P-CDs on ssDNA were also showed in Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of positive charged carbon dots (P-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared P-CDs were facilely synthesized via a pyrolysis strategy. Briefly, 2.0 g of citric acid monohydrate mixed with 0.6 g of L-glutathione (GSH) were placed in a 50 mL round bottom flask. Herein, citric acid and GSH were used as carbon source and doping element source, respectively [4]. After the mixture in flask was heated from room temperature to 180 °C in an oil bath, 10 mL of polyene polyamine (PEPA, CAS No. 68131-73-7) was added to the flask and kept at 180 °C for another 1 h. Later, the reaction flask was removed and cooled to room temperature naturally. Then, the cinnamon-colored, syrup-like product was dissolved with acetone, followed by centrifugation to obtain yellow precipitate. Then the dialysis was performed with a 2 kDa cutoff membrane for 2 days to purify the product. After dialysis, the yellow cotton candy-like mass of P-CDs was obtained from freeze-drying method. The P-CDs were redispersed into ultrapure water to be 500 μg/mL as a stock solution for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA detection assisted with exonuclease III (Exo III) amplification using P-CDs as nanoquencher
ce:section.ce:para: First, 50 nM P-DNA was mixed with P-CDs (6.25 μg/mL) for 30 min at room temperature. Then 100 U Exo III was added to the mixture of P-DNA, P-CDs and different concentrations of T-DNA in a total volume of 200 μL with Tris–HCl buffer. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 45 min and another 10 min at 75 °C to terminate the Exo III reaction. After that, the reaction system was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature before the fluorescence measurements. The fluorescence spectra of different samples were measured with an excitation wavelength at 488 nm with emission scan range from 510 nm to 632 nm for FAM (carboxyfluorescein, Fig. S1), while excitation wavelength at 345 nm with emission scan range from 378 nm to 508 nm for P-CDs.

P-CDs were synthesized via co-pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and glutathione (GSH), followed by the addition of polyene polyamine (PEPA) as passivation agent, as shown in Scheme 1A. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that the average diameter of P-CDs was 2.7 nm with a narrow size distribution (Fig. 1 A). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) revealed that the typical lattice spacing was 0.22 nm, ascribed to the (100) diffraction facet of graphite carbon [7]. The AFM image in Fig. 1B indicated that P-CDs were monodispersed, with uniform heights in the range of 2.0–3.0 nm. Therefore, we concluded that P-CDs are quasi-spherical. FTIR and XPS were conducted to investigate the structures and compositions of P-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1C, several polar groups are present (e.g., the stretching vibrations of OH and NH2 at 3424 and 3270 cm−1), thereby ensuring excellent dispersion and stability in water [31]. Moreover, the stretching vibration of CO (1651 cm−1), the bending vibration of NH (1566 cm−1), and the asymmetric stretching vibration of CNHC (1121 cm−1) are also observed, indicating the presence of doped N and the formation of rich amine (like NH2, amide II (NH) and −NH-) [32] on the surfaces of P-CDs. The full XPS spectrum in Fig. 1D depicts the doping effect of N and S in the prepared dots. High-resolution analysis of C1s, N1s, and S2p XPS peaks were also investigated. High-resolution C1 s analysis indicates peaks of CC/CC (graphitic or aliphatic carbon) at 284.6 eV, CO/CN/C-S (sp3 carbon) at 285.7 eV and CN/CO at 287.7 eV (Fig. S2A) [31,33]. The detailed XPS survey of N1 s (Fig. S2B) can be deconvoluted into two binding energies of 399.5 eV and 400.1 eV, corresponding to the formation of CNC and CNH2 [33,34]. Moreover, high resolution S2p analysis (Fig. S2C) obviously reveals peaks at 164.1 eV and 168.2 eV. The former peak at 164.1 eV is ascribed to the CSC covalent bond of thiophene-like S [34,35]. While the latter peak is consistent with the sulphone bridges of CS(O)2 C [35]. The XPS results are consistent with the characterization results by FTIR, thus confirming the molecular structure of P-CDs.

The optical properties, stability, and biocompatibility of P-CDs were further investigated. The UV–vis spectrum of P-CDs reveals two absorption peaks at 345 and 212 nm, which are ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO and the π-π* transition of CC in the aromatic ring structure, respectively (Fig. 1E). The solid state of P-CDs is fluffy and brown yellow (Fig. 1F). In solution, P-CDs show a luminescence wavelength maximum at 420 nm with 345 nm excitation (Fig. 1E), resulting in a bright blue color under UV lamp and yellow color in daylight (Fig. 1F). Similar to other carbon-based fluorescent materials [31,33,36], P-CDs exhibit an excitation-dependent luminescence behavior (Fig. S3). The QY of P-CDs was calculated as 12.78% when excited at 345 nm (Fig. S4). Contrastively, specific carbon dots prepared from pure citric acid (named as citric acid CDs) through direct pyrolysis strategy [37] without any doping elements or the passivation of PEPA have a lower QY of 6.96%, indicating the important role of the doping effect of N and S for the improvement of the optical property of carbon dots [37,38]. When P-CDs were stored under ordinary conditions, the luminescence stability exhibited almost no attenuation within the six-month observation period (Fig. S5A). Moreover, compared with conventional organic dyes, P-CDs maintain more than 80% of their original luminescent intensity under a continuous 60 min UV irradiation induced by a 300 W xenon lamp (Fig. S5B). MTT assays were conducted using the A549 cell line to evaluate the biocompatibility of P-CDs. The cells exhibited approximately 87% cell viabilities after a 24 h incubation with 50 μg/mL P-CDs, proving that P-CDs are low-toxicity (Fig. S6). Combined with the high fluorescent QY and excellent photo-stability, P-CDs can be a suitable fluorescent probe for bioassay.

In view of the surface functional groups, P-CDs were tried to be applied to establish a smart nanoquencher for dye-labeled DNA for use in sensing. First, different concentrations of P-CDs were incubated with P-DNA (Table S1). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of FAM at 520 nm gradually decreased as the concentration of P-CDs increased, achieving a minimum value at a P-CDs concentration of 3.13 μg/mL. The quenching efficiency (QE) was calculated to be 84.5%. Furthermore, the quenching of ssDNA labeled with other fluorophores, such as ROX, Cy5 and TAMRA, was also realized by P-CDs (Figs. 2B and S7). In addition, P-CDs can cause 82.5% quenching of ROX-labeled ATP-binding aptamer probe with a P-CDs content of 6.25 μg/mL (Fig. 2B). The kinetic behaviors of P-DNA and P-CDs (Fig. S8) revealed that the quenching of labeled-DNA by P-CDs was achieved within 1 min. We further investigated the ubiquitous quenching effect of P-CDs toward different DNAs with varied base lengths and compositions. Fig. 2C shows the close and high QEs of P-CDs for different DNA sequences of varied lengths and compositions. This characteristic is different from the DNA-length- and composition-dependent quenching efficiency of graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets [39]. However, P-CDs do not quench pure fluorophore, like FAM (Fig. 2D), suggesting that the anchoring of DNA onto P-CDs to induce the quenching behavior of FAM by P-CDs due to FRET between dyes labeled ssDNA and nanoquenchers [18,19]. Furthermore, considering the maximum emission wavelength of P-CDs centered at 420 nm and the maximum excitation wavelength of FAM at 488 nm, the possible FRET behavior [40] based on the dyes labeled DNA as an acceptor for P-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. S9, the system of P-CDs and FAM-DNA only has an emission peak centered at 420 nm when excited at 345 nm, which is almost the same as the fluorescence spectrum of single P-CDs. Furthermore, when excited at 345 nm, the similar phenomenon of only an emission peak ascribed to P-CDs is found in the system of P-CDs and Cy5 (TAMRA) labeled DNA (data not shown), respectively. The above results confirm that P-CDs can be an efficient carrier and quencher for dyes labeled ssDNA with the kept fluorescence of P-CDs. The above results exhibit that P-CDs possess considerably high quenching effects on a variety of DNAs and fluorophores, although P-CDs are smaller in size.

We examined the quenching mechanism of P-CDs toward dye-labeled DNA. P-CDs also quench P-DNA (∼76% QE) in pure water (Fig. 3 A). The surface abundant amine groups make P-CDs positively charged with a zeta-potential of 20.29 mV (Fig. 3B). A decreased zeta-potential of the mixture of P-CDs and ssDNA was determined as 7.23 mV. In addition, the possible quenching ability of citric acid CDs was compared (Fig. S10). The zeta-potential of citric acid CD was measured to be −1.65 mV due to some carboxylic groups on the surface of citric acid CDs [10]. It shows that even high concentration of citric acid CDs had only about 30% QE to FAM labeled ssDNA, suggesting the much weaker quenching ability of citric acid CDs than P-CDs. The results suggest that the electrostatic interaction for the binding and quenching of P-CDs to ssDNA existed due to the negative nature of DNA and positive charge of P-CDs. Notably, the reactive systems for ssDNA and P-CDs in Tris-HCl buffer contained NaCl and other ions, which can shield electrostatic interactions to some extent [41]. Considering that the QE of P-CDs to FAM-modified DNA is higher in buffer than in pure water, we propose that electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking interactions are jointly responsible for the quenching behavior [39]. The length of adjacent base pair plane is 0.34 nm, thus, a DNA nucleotide with 40 bases has a length of longer than 10 nm. A previous work reported that the quenching effect for the interaction between GO and DNA was dependent on nanometer size [39]. This finding raises the question as to how P-CDs with an average diameter of 2.7 nm quench long-length DNA.

The distribution of the mixture of P-CDs and ssDNA was investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3C, the introduction of negative ssDNA promoted the spontaneous aggregation of positive P-CDs (the circled section), thereby strengthening the total capacity of P-CDs to adsorb and quench long fluorophore-labeled ssDNA. The circular dichroism (CD) spectra were obtained to confirm the interaction of ssDNA with P-CDs, while the adsorption of ssDNA on GO was used as a characterized reference (Fig. 3D). ssDNA shows an obvious negative peak at 250 nm, which corresponded to helicity, and a positive peak at 280 nm, due to base stacking, suggesting a helical structure of ssDNA [42,43]. The conformation of ssDNA gradually changes as the P-CDs content increases. The negative peak at 250 nm weakens and even becomes positive at high concentrations of P-CDs. Moreover, the original positive peak at 280 nm of ssDNA was blueshifted to approximately 272 nm when P-CDs were introduced. The adsorption of ssDNA on P-CDs was confirmed by CD spectra, which mirror the peak shapes of the system of ssDNA on GO.

The affinities of P-CDs to ssDNA and dsDNA were also investigated (Fig. 4 A). The quenched ssDNA (P-DNA) recovered fluorescence through the addition of the complementary sequence, indicating the different anchoring abilities of P-CDs toward ssDNA and dsDNA, implying the application prospect of the system of ssDNA and P-CDs for gene detection. Fluorescence anisotropy measurement, a powerful tool for molecular interaction probing between DNA and nanomaterials [44,45], was carried out to investigate the anchoring ability of P-CDs to ssDNA and dsDNA. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence anisotropy of ssDNA was greatly enhanced by P-CDs, suggesting the strong interaction between ssDNA and P-CDs. The fluorescence anisotropy of dsDNA in the presence or absence of P-CDs was close, indicating that the interaction between dsDNA and P-CDs was weak [44,45]. Thus, the formation of dsDNA through hybridization can make the quenched P-DNA leave from the surface of P-CDs, leading to recover of fluorescence.

Fig. S9 illustrated that P-CDs maintain stable luminescence properties, both in the absence and presence of ssDNA. Thus P-CDs can serve as an internal stable reference signals for the design of a ratiometric fluorescent method. By using P-CDs as quenching carriers for P-DNA and Exo III for the step removal of mononucleotides from 3′ to 5′ in dsDNA, enzyme-assisted target DNA (T-DNA) recycling was obtained with improved sensitivity [19]. When P-CDs are introduced, they serve as an internal reference for FAM and a protective platform for the possible nonspecific enzymatic digestion (Fig. S11 and the corresponding supplementary information) [46]. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of FAM (FFAM) increased gradually with the concentration of target DNA, but the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs (FP-CDs) at 420 nm remained almost unchanged. Using the ratio of FFAM to FP-CDs, a linear relationship of FFAM/FP-CDs and T-DNA in the range of 0.05–1 nM was obtained. The calibration equation is FFAM/FP-CDs = 0.422C + 0.668 (R2 = 0.997), with a detection limit of 34 pM based on 3σ/slope (Fig. 5B), which is comparable or even better than several nanoquencher-based fluorescence methods for DNA/RNA with or without amplification techniques [18,47–50]. Moreover, the proposed ratiometric method can only present negligible fluorescence response towards different mismatched DNAs containing single-base mismatched DNA (Fig. S12), indicating excellent selectivity.

Furthermore, the reproducibility of this strategy for numerous testing was also studied. The comparison of 30 individual measurements of 0.05 nM T-DNA in 5 separate days by non-ratiometric and ratiometric strategies was performed through the reported means of manipulating data [22]. As shown in Table S3 and Fig. 6 , the SDs and RSDs of the measurement were 0.034 and 0.045 through the proposed ratiometric strategy, respectively, which were lower than the results through non-ratiometric strategy. The results indicate the improved reproducibility and reliability of the proposed method using P-CDs as nanoquencher and internal reference rather than using the “absolute values” of fluorescence intensity [22,51].

In summary, small-sized P-CDs with positive charged surface were prepared by a simple method. P-CDs retain stable inherent fluorescence when used as nanoquencher for fluorophore-modified DNA via electrostatic and π-π stacking interactions. Furthermore, the assembly of P-CDs induced by ssDNA promotes the quenching behavior. By utilizing the interaction of the target with the DNA probe, new applications based on a ratiometric strategy were fabricated for the analysis of genes using the stable luminescence of P-CDs as an internal reference. The analytical method exhibits improved reproducibility with acceptable sensitivity and simplicity for DNA sensing. We believe that this system will expand the general scope of nanoquenchers and dye-labeled ssDNA for wider biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405016, 81641097), the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2015J01043, 2015J01475, 2016J01767), the Major Program of Medical and Health Foundation of Nanjing Military Region (12Z39), the Elite Cultivation Program of Health and Family Planning of Fujian Province (2017-ZQN-61), and Opening Foundation of the State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces (Xiamen University) (201517).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.175.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots doped with heteroatoms for fluorescent bioimaging: a review
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Hierarchically targeted and penetrated delivery of drugs to tumors by size-changeable graphene quantum dot nanoaircrafts for photolytic therapy

Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications

Detection of microRNA by electrocatalytic amplification: a general approach for single-particle biosensing
Graphdiyne oxide as a platform for fluorescence sensing
Biosensors based on graphene oxide and its biomedical application
Gold nanoparticles for biology and medicine

An MnO2 nanosheet as a label-free nanoplatform for homogeneous biosensing
DNA adsorption by indium tin oxide nanoparticles
Polydopamine nanotubes as an effective fluorescent quencher for highly sensitive and selective detection of biomolecules assisted with exonuclease III amplification
Carbon nanomaterials and DNA: from molecular recognition to applications
Recent advances in ultrathin two-dimensional nanomaterials
A ratiometric fluorescent bioprobe for highly reproducible detection of telomerase in bloody urines of bladder cancer patients
Synthesis of molecularly imprinted carbon dot grafted YVO4: Eu3+ for the ratiometric fluorescent determination of paranitrophenol
Sequence and structure dual-dependent interaction between small molecules and DNA for the detection of residual silver ions in as-prepared silver nanomaterials
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
Unique approach to develop carbon dot-based nanohybrid near-infrared ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of mercury ions
Reagentless, ratiometric electrochemical DNA sensors with improved robustness and reproducibility

Ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of nucleic acids based on the dual-signaling electrochemical ratiometric method and exonuclease III-assisted target recycling amplification strategy
Universal ratiometric electrochemical biosensing platform based on mesoporous platinum nanocomposite and nicking endonuclease assisted DNA walking strategy
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
Carbon nanodot-sensitized modulation of Alzheimer’s β-amyloid self-assembly, disassembly, and toxicity
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning sensors, and bioimaging
Multifunctional N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots with pH- and thermo-dependent switchable fluorescent properties and highly selective detection of glutathione

Highly Fe3+-selective fluorescent nanoprobe based on ultrabright N/P codoped carbon dots and its application in biological samples
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots as selective fluorescent probes for phosphate-containing metabolites
Size-dependent programming of the dynamic range of graphene oxide-DNA interaction-based ion sensors
A novel water-soluble quantum dot-neutral red fluorescence resonance energy transfer probe for the selective detection of megestrol acetate
Freezing directed construction of bio/nano interfaces: reagentless conjugation, denser spherical nucleic acids, and better nanoflares
Carbon nanotubes selective destabilization of duplex and triplex DNA and inducing B-A transition in solution
Carboxyl-modified single-walled carbon nanotubes selectively induce human telomeric i-motif formation
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.
A graphene oxide enhanced fluorescence anisotropy strategy for DNAzyme-based assay of metal ions
A graphene platform for sensing biomolecules
Rapid and label-free monitoring of exonuclease III-assisted target recycling amplification
Fluorescence quenching of carbon nitride nanosheet through its interaction with DNA for versatile fluorescence sensing
High-yield exfoliation of ultrathin two-dimensional ternary chalcogenide nanosheets for highly sensitive and selectivefluorescence DNA sensors
Ultrathin two-dimensional covalent organic framework nanosheets: preparation and application in highly sensitive and selective DNA detection
An enzyme-free DNA circuit-assisted graphene oxide enhanced fluorescence anisotropy assay for microRNA detection with improved sensitivity and selectivity
Ratiometric electrochemical immunoassay based on internal reference value for reproducible and sensitive detection of tumor marker

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rubin Guo received his BS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2015. He is currently an MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. His research interests are pharmaceutical analysis and fluorescence analysis.

Bing Chen received his BS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2013. He is currently a PhD graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. His research interest is pharmaceutical analysis.

Fenglan Li received her BS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2016. She is currently an MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. Her research interests are pharmaceutical analysis and fluorescence analysis.

Shaohuang Weng earned his PhD degree at the Department of Chemistry, Xiamen University, in 2009. He is an Associate professor in the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University. From 2016–2017, he did his research about the DNA biosensor in Xiamen University as a visiting scholar. His research focuses on preparation and application of nanomaterials in bioanalysis.

Zongfu Zheng is Associate Chief Physician in Department of Laboratory Medicine, 476 hospital of PLA. He received his BS and MS degree in Medicine from FujianMedical University in 1992 and 2007, respectively. His current research interests include new technologies of clinical diagnosis and novel drug carriers.

Min Chen is Associate Chief Physician in Department of Orthopedic surgery, Affiliated Union Hospital of Fujian Medical University. He received his BS and PhD degree in clinical medicine from Fujian Medical University in 1998 and 2013, respectively. His research interests include the clinical diagnosis and treatment of joint diseases and biosensors.

Wen Wu received his BS degree in clinical medicine from Xiamen University in 2015. He is currently an MS graduate student in clinical medicine in Fujian Medical University. His research interests include the clinical diagnosis and treatment of joint diseases and biosensors.

Xinhua Lin is a Professor of Analytical Chemistry in Fujian Medical University. He received his BS and MS degrees in Analytical Chemistry from Xiamen University, China, in 1983 and 1989, respectively. He is also a doctoral supervisor. His research interest is biosensor.

Chaoyong Yang is a Professor of Chemical Biology in Xiamen University. He received his PhD degree in Chemical Biology from Department of Chemistry, University of Florida, USA, in 2006. He is also a doctoral supervisor. His research interests are Molecular Engineering, Molecular Evolution and Microfluidics.
